name: pytz
what_it_does: Timezone library for Python datetime — provides Olson timezone database for datetime localization and conversion. pytz features: pytz.timezone() for timezone objects, datetime.astimezone() for conversion, pytz.localize() for naive datetime localization, pytz.utc for UTC timezone, pytz.all_timezones for all timezone names, pytz.common_timezones for common zones, and normalization after arithmetic. Predates Python 3.9 zoneinfo module. Now largely superseded by stdlib zoneinfo but widely used in existing codebases.
use_cases: Agent UTC storage — from datetime import datetime; import pytz; utc = pytz.utc; now_utc = datetime.now(utc); stored = now_utc.isoformat() — always store as UTC; agent converts to local for display; pytz.utc is the recommended UTC timezone object for stdlib datetime

Agent timezone conversion — eastern = pytz.timezone('US/Eastern'); utc_time = datetime(2024, 6, 15, 12, 0, 0, tzinfo=pytz.utc); eastern_time = utc_time.astimezone(eastern) — convert UTC to local timezone; agent displays timestamps in user's timezone; astimezone() handles DST transitions automatically

Agent datetime localization — eastern = pytz.timezone('US/Eastern'); naive_dt = datetime(2024, 3, 15, 12, 0, 0); localized = eastern.localize(naive_dt) — localize naive datetime; agent receives naive datetime from external system; localize() makes it timezone-aware; do not use replace(tzinfo=eastern) — wrong for DST boundary times

Agent timezone list — user_tz = request.json.get('timezone'); if user_tz not in pytz.all_timezones_set: return error; tz = pytz.timezone(user_tz) — validate user-provided timezone; agent user preference validation uses pytz.all_timezones_set for O(1) membership check; pytz.common_timezones for UI dropdown

Agent DST-aware scheduling — eastern = pytz.timezone('US/Eastern'); scheduled = eastern.localize(datetime(2024, 11, 3, 9, 0)); utc_scheduled = scheduled.astimezone(pytz.utc) — 9 AM Eastern before/after DST change is different UTC times; agent scheduler computes correct UTC for 'same local time' after DST change via localize
not_for: New Python 3.9+ code — use stdlib zoneinfo module which is simpler and has no pitfalls; pytz is legacy but maintained
Datetime arithmetic with pytz — adding timedelta to pytz-localized datetime can produce wrong result without normalize(); use pendulum or arrow for safe arithmetic
Modern Django/SQLAlchemy — both now support zoneinfo natively; pytz still works but zoneinfo is preferred
best_when: Maintaining existing Python codebases that use pytz for timezone handling — pytz remains correct and maintained but new code should use Python 3.9+ zoneinfo instead.
avoid_when: Starting new projects on Python 3.9+ (use zoneinfo), need safe timezone arithmetic (use pendulum), or want minimal dependencies.

pricing.notes: pytz is MIT licensed. Free for all use.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits. pytz.timezone() lookup: ~1μs. localize() and astimezone(): ~5μs. Suitable for real-time use. pytz caches timezone objects internally.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: pytz.exceptions.UnknownTimeZoneError for invalid timezone names. NonExistentTimeError for DST gap times. AmbiguousTimeError for DST fall-back times. Error messages include the invalid timezone name. pytz errors are clear for common issues.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: pytz timezone operations are deterministic. localize() and astimezone() produce consistent results. pytz.timezone() returns same object for same name (cached). Timezone conversions are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Never use replace(tzinfo=tz) — datetime(2024, 1, 1).replace(tzinfo=eastern) uses historical EST offset not LMT; correct: eastern.localize(datetime(2024, 1, 1)); agent code using replace() instead of localize() gets wrong UTC offset for many timezones; pytz documentation explicitly warns against replace() for pytz timezones

Datetime arithmetic requires normalize() — localized = eastern.localize(datetime(2024, 3, 10, 1, 0)); wrong = localized + timedelta(hours=1); correct = eastern.normalize(localized + timedelta(hours=1)) — adding timedelta crosses DST boundary without normalize() gives wrong timezone offset; use pendulum or arrow for safe arithmetic; pytz normalize() fixes the offset after arithmetic

AmbiguousTimeError during DST fall-back — eastern.localize(datetime(2024, 11, 3, 1, 30)) raises AmbiguousTimeError — 1:30 AM exists twice on fall-back day; must specify: eastern.localize(datetime(2024, 11, 3, 1, 30), is_dst=True) for first occurrence or is_dst=False for second; agent scheduling during fall-back must explicitly handle ambiguity

NonExistentTimeError during DST spring-forward — eastern.localize(datetime(2024, 3, 10, 2, 30)) raises NonExistentTimeError — 2:30 AM doesn't exist (clocks spring to 3:00); agent must handle: try: eastern.localize(dt); except pytz.NonExistentTimeError: use is_dst=True to interpret as spring forward equivalent

pytz is de-prioritized in Python 3.9+ — Python 3.9 added zoneinfo module covering pytz's functionality; datetime(2024, 1, 1, tzinfo=ZoneInfo('America/New_York')) is the modern approach; agent new code should use from zoneinfo import ZoneInfo; pytz remains correct but zoneinfo is stdlib-native

pytz timezone objects are not equal to zoneinfo objects — pytz.timezone('UTC') != datetime.timezone.utc; mixing pytz and zoneinfo objects raises TypeError in comparisons; agent codebase mixing old (pytz) and new (zoneinfo) code must normalize to one timezone library; convert pytz tz to zoneinfo: ZoneInfo(str(pytz_tz))

security_score_components.security_notes: Timezone library with no network calls. Incorrect timezone handling (wrong DST, naive datetimes) can cause security issues in time-sensitive agent operations (token expiry, session management). Always use timezone-aware datetimes for security-critical time comparisons.
